coredata.dc:title: Structure and performance insights in La modified carbon quantum dots-epoxy coating with efficiency corrosion and wear resistance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zeng, Qi
Yin, Haitao
Li, Qunfang
Bulyk, Ihor I.
Wang, Zhixiang
Zhou, Shengguo
coredata.dc:description: Epoxy resin is a very versatile material but has limited corrosion and wear resistance. Carbon quantum dots are a new type of corrosion inhibitor material with good dispersibility. In this paper, lanthanum nitrate, ethylenediamine and citric acid were mixed to prepare lanthanum‑nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (La/N-CQDs) by a hydrothermal method, which could show good dispersion and compatibility in epoxy resin. Meanwhile, a series of La/N-CQDs modified La/N-CQDs@EP coatings with long-term corrosion resistance and friction reduction were successfully prepared on the NdFeB surface by spin-coating method. The results of electrochemical tests and tribological tests show that the La/N-CQDs doped EP composite coatings could effectively enhance the anti-corrosion and anti-wear protection to the NdFeB surface. The La/N-CQDs@EP composite coating doped with 2 wt% La/N-CQDs showed the lowest corrosion current density and remained the highest impedance modulus after 14 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. While the La/N-CQDs@EP composite coating doped with 1.5 wt% La/N-CQDs possess relatively low friction coefficient and wear rate. The excellent corrosion protection and wear resistance of the La/N-CQDs@EP coatings were mainly due to the enhanced interfacial bonding, and improved the densification of the epoxy resin structure. These indicated that La/N-CQDs modified EP composite coatings could effectively control the corrosion and wear loss of NdFeB in some harsh environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: La/N-CQDs
Epoxy resin
NdFeB
Corrosion and wear

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface morphology after peeling off the coatings

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of the preparation processes for La/N-CQDs and La/N-CQDs@EP coating.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of La/N-CQDs. (b) UV–vis spectra of La/N-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of La/N-CQDs. (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s and (e) La 3d XPS spectra of La/N-CQDs.
TEM image of La/N-CQDs. Digital images of La/N-CQDs dispersion.
SEM surface images of (a) pure EP, (b) La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, (c) La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, (d) La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, (e) La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and (f) La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings.
Equivalent circuit model fitted with the simple (a) R(QR), (b) R(QR(QR)) and (c) R(Q(R(Q(RW)))) electrical models.
EIS of different immersion time: (a), (b) 1 day; (c), (d) 3 day; (e), (f) 5 day; (g), (h) 7 day; (i), (j) 14 day.
Tafel curves of pure EP, La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings and uncoating.

SEM and EDS images of the corrosion products for (a) pure EP, (b) La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, (c) La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, (d) La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, (e) La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and (f) La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings for immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl environment for 14 days.

SEM images of corrosion region on NdFeB surface under different coatings: (a) pure EP, (b) La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, (c) La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, (d) La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, (e) La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and (f) La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings.

Roughness of corrosion region on NdFeB surface under different coatings: (a) pure EP, (b) La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, (c) La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, (d) La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, (e) La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and (f) La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings.
Schematic of corrosion protection mechanism for pure EP (a, c) and La/N-CQDs@EP (b, d).

SEM images of abrasion surface for coatings with different La/N-CQDs contents. (a) pure EP, (b) La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, (c) La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, (d) La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, (e) La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and (f) La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings.
(a) COF curves for coatings with different La/N-CQDs contents, (b) Two-dimensional profile curves of coating with La/N-CQDs different contents.
Friction coefficient and specific wear rate values of coatings prepared in different La/N-CQDs concentrations.
Schematic of friction mechanism for (a) pure EP and (b) La/N-CQDs@EP.
Worn surfaces and EDS images of (a) pure EP, (b) La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, (c) La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, (d) La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, (e) La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and (f) La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings.
EIS fitting parameters for different concentrations of La/N-CQDs@EP coatings.
Corrosion parameters of NdFeB in 3.5 wt% NaCl solutions with different contents of La/N-CQDs@EP coatings.

head.ce:title: Structure and performance insights in La modified carbon quantum dots-epoxy coating with efficiency corrosion and wear resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematics, Ganzhou Teachers College, Ganzhou 341000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mathematics, Ganzhou Teachers College, Ganzhou, 341000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Rare Earth Functional Materials Innovation Center, Ganzhou 341000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Rare Earth Functional Materials Innovation Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Rare Earth Functional Materials Innovation Center, Ganzhou,341000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Epoxy resin is a very versatile material but has limited corrosion and wear resistance. Carbon quantum dots are a new type of corrosion inhibitor material with good dispersibility. In this paper, lanthanum nitrate, ethylenediamine and citric acid were mixed to prepare lanthanum‑nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (La/N-CQDs) by a hydrothermal method, which could show good dispersion and compatibility in epoxy resin. Meanwhile, a series of La/N-CQDs modified La/N-CQDs@EP coatings with long-term corrosion resistance and friction reduction were successfully prepared on the NdFeB surface by spin-coating method. The results of electrochemical tests and tribological tests show that the La/N-CQDs doped EP composite coatings could effectively enhance the anti-corrosion and anti-wear protection to the NdFeB surface. The La/N-CQDs@EP composite coating doped with 2 wt% La/N-CQDs showed the lowest corrosion current density and remained the highest impedance modulus after 14 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. While the La/N-CQDs@EP composite coating doped with 1.5 wt% La/N-CQDs possess relatively low friction coefficient and wear rate. The excellent corrosion protection and wear resistance of the La/N-CQDs@EP coatings were mainly due to the enhanced interfacial bonding, and improved the densification of the epoxy resin structure. These indicated that La/N-CQDs modified EP composite coatings could effectively control the corrosion and wear loss of NdFeB in some harsh environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern civilization, electrical, electronic, and electromechanical devices with magnetic materials as core components are ubiquitous in industry and daily life. Permanent magnetic materials based on rare earth elements and transition metal intermetallic compounds exhibit excellent magnetic properties. Among them, sintered NdFeB, as the strongest known permanent magnet, has many advantages and is widely used [1]. In many products, due to the requirement for strong permanent magnet properties, other types of magnets have mostly been replaced by NdFeB. Inevitably, NdFeB materials are used in corrosive environments, such as industries involving acidic media and marine environments [2,3]. Due to the high reactivity of the rare earth element Nd and the transition metal Fe, NdFeB magnets are mainly composed of the Nd2Fe14B matrix phase, Nd-rich phase, and B-rich phase [4]. The unique phase structure of NdFeB magnets results in many tiny voids and gaps, with significant electrochemical potential differences between the phases [5].Corrosive media preferentially react with the highly active Nd-rich phase, causing intergranular corrosion, damaging the magnet structure, and reducing magnetic performance [6]. Therefore, it is essential to take appropriate measures to enhance the corrosion resistance of NdFeB magnets.

Currently, methods to protect NdFeB materials from corrosion are mainly divided into alloying and surface treatment. Alloying involves doping small amounts of representative alloy elements such as Dy, Ce, Cu, Al, Co, and Ti during the sintering process of NdFeB magnets to optimize the magnet structure and reduce the potential differences between the phases [6–9]. Although this improves the corrosion resistance of NdFeB magnets, the addition of non-magnetic phases reduces the magnetic performance and increases production costs. Therefore, the mainstream approach is to enhance the corrosion resistance of NdFeB magnets through surface treatments. Conventional surface treatments include electroplating, plasma deposition, and organic coating preparation. Electroplating with metals such as Ni, Ni-Co, Al, Zn, and Al-Zn is an effective protection method [6,10–12], but it has drawbacks such as environmental pollution and potential damage to the magnetic properties of NdFeB magnets. Plasma deposition could produce high-quality films [13,14], but it has a small batch size, long production cycle, low efficiency, and high precision equipment requirements, significantly increasing manufacturing costs, making it unsuitable for industrial production. Epoxy and composite coatings have garnered significant attention from researchers due to their exceptional chemical and heat resistance, low shrinkage, electrical insulation properties, and strong adhesion to various substrates [15,16]. However, during the curing process of epoxy resin, small voids inevitably form, creating defects that act as preferential channels for corrosion media, thereby increasing the coating's susceptibility to failure under friction. To enhance the performance of these coatings, researchers often incorporate nanofillers into the epoxy resin [17]. Ye et al. [18] utilized citric acid derivatives to prepare functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which were then used to develop a CQDs-modified graphene/epoxy resin coating, significantly improving the barrier properties of the epoxy coating. Cheng et al. [19] prepared a carbon fiber‑carbon nanotube multiscale hybrid material using a non-corrosive LaCl3 surface treatment method. The incorporation of carbon fiber‑carbon nanotubes into the composite significantly reduced both the friction coefficient and wear rate of the epoxy composite. Fan et al. [20] constructed a multilayer multifunctional epoxy composite coating consisting of a bottom layer of nano-Al2O3/epoxy, an intermediate G/epoxy layer, and a top layer of nano-SiO2/epoxy through a layer-by-layer assembly method, which improved the coating's corrosion resistance, UV stability, and tribological performance. Hu et al. [21] developed a novel CQDs/polyimide composite material using citric acid as a precursor, which significantly enhanced the tribological performance of polyimide.

In recent years, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have attracted increasing attention from researchers due to their high water solubility, low toxicity, simple synthesis process, and low preparation cost [22]. It has been reported that N-CQDs possess excellent physicochemical properties and a layered structure [23]. When added to polymers, N-CQDs could act as corrosion inhibitors and lubricants, effectively mitigating corrosion and frictional failure of coatings [24,25]. Atchudan et al. [26] and Song et al. [27] demonstrated that the amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs could coordinate with Fe3+ ions to form stable complexes. Researchers have also reported that N-CQDs contain charged sites, which facilitate the adsorption of O2 and weaken the OO bond, potentially enhancing the corrosion resistance of the coating [28–30]. Zhao et al. [31] prepared CQDs via a one-pot pyrolysis method using ammonium citrate as a carbon source. The resulting CQDs exhibited excellent hydrophilicity, good dispersant stability, and a lower friction coefficient (COF) and wear rate, while also demonstrating good corrosion inhibition properties. Doping or co-doping with heteroatoms is an effective strategy for modifying the electronic density of CQDs and imparting excellent performance, often leading to remarkable physical, chemical, and optical properties [32]. However, research on doping rare earth elements into carbon quantum dots remains limited. Rare earth salts, especially those of cerium and lanthanum, have been extensively studied. Results showed that lanthanide ions form insoluble hydroxides, which suppress the cathodic reaction of corrosion and protect metals from degradation [33–35]. Studies also indicate that coating surface films containing rare earth elements on alloys could improve their corrosion resistance in aqueous environments [36].

In this study, a novel La/N-CQDs was firstly prepared by combining N-CQDs with La (NO3)3 using a hydrothermal method. The La/N-CQDs could exhibit good dispersion in water, wide raw material sources, low cost, and excellent corrosion resistance. A simple spin-coating method was designed to prepare a series of La/N-CQDs modified La/N-CQDs@EP composite coatings on the surface of NdFeB. The structure and composition of as-synthesized La/N-CQDs were evaluated using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, XPS, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The corrosion resistance of as-prepared La/N-CQDs modified La/N-CQDs@EP composite coatings was tested using EIS and Tafel measurements. The NdFeB surface after corrosion tests were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and scanning probe microscopy (SPM). The friction and wear properties of the La/N-CQDs@EP composite coatings were tested using a reciprocating friction and wear testing machine, and the cross-sections of wear tracks were observed using a two-dimensional optical profiler.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, lanthanum nitrate, and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Epoxy resin E51 and polyamide curing agent were purchased from Kunshan Jiulimei Electronic Materials Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). NdFeB magnet N40 (20 × 20 × 10 mm) was purchased from Dongtai Rongxinyuan Magnetic Industry Co., Ltd. (Jiangsu, China). Before electrochemical measurement, NdFeB was first polished with 400–1200 mesh emery papers, and then washed with ultrapure water and ethanol.

La/N-CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ) [21]. First, 2 g citric acid monohydrate, 500 μL ethylenediamine, and 0.6 g lanthanum nitrate were dissolved in 20 mL distilled water and stirred for 20 min to obtain a clear solution. Then, the solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. In the third step, the brown-black transparent product was filtered through a 0.1 μm syringe filter and further purified in a dialysis bag (500 Da) with deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the dispersion was freeze-dried in a freeze dryer to obtain La/N-CQDs powder. The La/N-CQDs synthesized by this method are environmentally friendly, low-cost, and have a yield that could reach the gram level.

The general procedure for preparing La/N-CQDs@EP coatings is as follows (Fig. 1). Firstly, La/N-CQDs powder (0.5 % of the total mass of epoxy resin and hardener) was dispersed in a small amount of deionized water and sonicated for 10 min. Next, 1.5 g of curing agent was added to the dispersion, which was then sonicated for 30 min and rotary evaporated for 60 min. Third, 5 g epoxy resin was added and stirred with the above mixture for 20 min. Finally, the mixture was evenly distributed on the surface of NdFeB by spin coating. The prepared coating was cured at 40 °C for 24 h. Additionally, epoxy composite coatings containing 1, 1.5, 2, and 2.5 wt% La/N-CQDs were prepared following the same procedure for comparison, and were named La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings, respectively. The final dry thickness of all coatings was 25 ± 3 μm.

The functional groups of La/N-CQDs were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Alpha BRUKER, Germany), ultraviolet spectroscopy (UV–Vis, UV-2550, Japan), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000, Japan). The dispersion of La/N-CQDs in water was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-F200, Japan). The morphology of La/N-CQDs doped epoxy resin coatings and their corrosion morphology were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Carl Zeiss Evo18, Japan). Electrochemical analysis was performed using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) with a traditional three-electrode system, consisting of a working electrode (exposed area of 1 cm2), a platinum sheet electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were conducted with a sine wave perturbation of 20 mV in the frequency range of 105–10−2 Hz. The three-dimensional surface morphology was analyzed using a scanning probe microscope (SPM). The friction and wear properties of the materials were evaluated using a reciprocating friction and wear testing machine (MFT-R4000, China) with a sliding length of 5 mm, a normal load of 5 N, a sliding time of 30 min, and a sliding frequency of 5 Hz. The two-dimensional profiles of the wear tracks were observed using a two-dimensional optical profiler (MT-500, Lanzhou, China). The Coating Adhesion Scratch Tester tests (WS-2005, Lanzhou, China) the bond between the coating and the substrate using acoustic emission measurements under a 20 N dynamic load.

The formation process of La/N-CQDs involves first mixing citric acid, ethylenediamine, and lanthanum nitrate, followed by a hydrothermal synthesis to produce polymer-like La/N-CQDs, these particles, approximately 10 nm in size, exhibit excellent fluorescence under a handheld UV lamp. Which are then freeze-dried to form powder-like La/N-CQDs. FT-IR and XPS confirmed that the surface of La/N-CQDs contains highly oxygenated and nitrogenated functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, amino, amide, and epoxy groups [27,28]. After ionization in water, these negatively charged groups give La/N-CQDs strong electrostatic repulsion to overcome van der Waals forces and hydrophobic forces [29]. When dissolved in deionized water, La/N-CQDs form a brown transparent dispersion, displaying excellent dispersibility after simple ultrasonic treatment in water and showing a clear Tyndall effect when a laser beam passes through [31].

The chemical structures of La/N-CQDs were characterized by FTIR, UV–vis and XPS spectroscopies, and the results were shown in Fig. 2a. From the FTIR spectra, it could be seen that a number of functional groups were presented in the La/N-CQDs, including CN vibration (1350 cm−1), CN vibration (1600 cm−1), CO vibration (1672 cm−1), CH vibration (2966 cm−1) and OH/NH vibration (3434 cm−1) (Fig. 2b) [32]. Among them, the formation of NCO bond indicated the successful reaction of the amino group of ethylenediamine with the carboxyl group of citric acid, and the weak band at 1050 cm−1 could be attributed to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of LaOH bond. The strong absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 was attributed to the vibrational mode of NO3 −1 anion caused by the residue of the synthesized raw material La (NO3)3·6H2O [37].The UV–vis mapping showed that the two characteristic absorption peaks appearing in the La/N-CQDs were mainly centered at ~338 nm and ~237 nm. They were related to the n-π* jumps corresponding to N-rich amine groups on the surface and the π-π* jumps of aromatic CC bonds, respectively [38].

The survey scan of La/N-CQDs was presented in Fig. 3 . Four peaks were observed at 284.8 eV, 400.3 eV, 531.7 eV and 837.9 eV, respectively, and were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and La 3d [35,37]. The analysis of the XPS data demonstrated that in the in the high-resolution region of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3a), four peaks were observed at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, 287.8 eV and 289.6 eV. These peaks were attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO and CN, respectively. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) could be fitted to three peaks at 399.4 eV, 400.3 eV and 401.5 eV, indicating the presence of three types of nitrogen bonds in La/N-CQDs: CN, NH and CN [38]. As illustrated in Fig. 3d, the O 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 531.68 eV, 530.98 eV, and 532.88 eV, which were ascribed to OLa, OC, and ON groups, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the La 3d orbital was presented in Fig. 3e, which illustrated the spin-orbit splitting of La 3d5/2 and La 3d3/2. The two distinctive peaks at 834.4 eV and 837.9 eV were ascribed to La 3d5/2, whereas the other two peaks at 851.9 eV and 855.4 eV correspond to La 3d3/2 and were attributed to the LaO and LaO groups [36,37].

To investigate the well-defined microscopic morphology of La/N-CQDs more effectively, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to analyze their dispersion state (Fig. 4 ). The TEM images reveal a significant number of well-dispersed nanoparticles with an approximate particle size of 10 nm and distinct lattice stripes, indicating that the carbon quantum dots were uniformly dispersed in deionized water.

Numerous studies have indicated that the rich and diverse functional groups (amine, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid) present on the surface of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots could coordinate with Fe3+ to form stable N-CQD@Fe3+ complexes [18]. To assess the corrosion inhibition capability of La/N-CQDs, EIS was employed to examine the corrosion behavior of NdFeB in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing 200 mg/L of La/N-CQDs (Fig. S1). The EIS data were analyzed with ZSimpWin software utilizing the electrical equivalent circuits depicted in Fig. 6. These circuits consist of solution resistance (Rs), film resistance (Rc), and charge-transfer resistance (Rct). Additionally, Qc and Qdl are constant phase elements used to replace the film capacitance and double-layer capacitance, correcting for deviations from ideal capacitance [20]. The fitting results were presented in Tables S1-S2.

As shown in Fig. S1, the impedance modulus at 0.01 Hz (|Z| 0.01 Hz), the breakpoint frequency (fb), and the capacitive loops for NdFeB immersed in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of La/N-CQDs were consistently higher than those for samples in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without La/N-CQDs. Notably, the Rct value increased to approximately 2062 Ω·cm2 upon the addition of 200 mg/L of La/N-CQDs. Furthermore, the Rc and Qdl values for NdFeB immersed in the NaCl solution with 200 mg/L of La/N-CQDs were significantly higher and lower, respectively, compared to those observed in the solution without La/N-CQDs, supporting the previous EIS test findings. These results indicated that the surface of NdFeB in the 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing 200 mg/L of La/N-CQDs was effectively covered by a protective film, thereby shielding the NdFeB from corrosive environments.

The polarization curves of NdFeB after immersion in this solution were recorded. Relevant corrosion parameters, including corrosion potential (E
corr
), corrosion current density (i
corr ), anode and cathode Tafel slopes (βa and βc), surface aging degree (θ), and inhibition efficiency (η), were summarized in Table S3. The values of θ and η were derived from i
corr using the following formula [21]:
(1)
θ =
i corr 0
−
i corr ′
i corr 0
(2)
η =
i corr 0
−
i corr ′
i corr 0
× 100 %
where
i corr 0
and
i corr ′
are the corrosion current densities in the absence and presence of La/N-CQDs, respectively.

This phenomenon could be attributed to the ability of La/N-CQDs to adsorb onto the surface of NdFeB, forming a robust protective film through the interactions between heteroatoms with free electrons (N and O) in La/N-CQDs and the unoccupied 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions. Consequently, this process restricted the mass transfer of corrosive media (H2O, O2, and Cl−) to the cathodic sites of NdFeB, thereby decreasing its corrosion rate.

The dispersion and compatibility of nanofillers within the epoxy matrix significantly influence the anticorrosive properties of the coatings. These properties were analyzed through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) observations of the surfaces of both pure EP coatings and composite coatings. The results indicated that the surface of the pure EP coatings exhibited greater porosity and a more loosely structured morphology in comparison, potentially due to weakened cross-linking between the epoxy resin and the curing agent, as well as void defects created by water evaporation during the curing process. Following the addition of a small amount of La/N-CQDs to the epoxy matrix, a notable reduction in the number of micropores on the surface of the composite coating was observed, resulting in a smooth and flat appearance compared to the pure EP coating (Fig. 5 ). However, excessive addition of La/N-CQDs led to the agglomeration of carbon quantum dots within the epoxy resin, which in turn caused a decrease in the structural density of the coatings and resulted in surface undulations. Hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, and epoxy groups present on the surface of La/N-CQDs could form hydrogen and covalent bonds with both the epoxy matrix and neighboring La/N-CQDs [26]. Additionally, van der Waals forces exist between adjacent La/N-CQDs due to their aromatic heterocyclic structure. This suggests that bonding interactions between neighboring La/N-CQDs, as well as those between La/N-CQDs and the epoxy matrix, contribute to a denser epoxy network [28]. Furthermore, the addition of La/N-CQDs was confirmed to enhance the crosslink density of the composite coatings, as evidenced by the surface morphology of the coatings after immersion.

The adhesion strength of the coating is an important indicator for evaluating its corrosion resistance. It was generally believed that the greater the adhesion strength of the coating, the better its corrosion resistance. Fig. S3 showed the adhesion strength of the prepared composite coatings. Clearly, the adhesion strength of the composite coating doped with La/N-CQDs is higher than that of the pure EP coating. As the doping content of La/N-CQDs increases, the adhesion strength between the composite coating and the substrate gradually increases, with the highest adhesion strength observed in the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating. This phenomenon was attributed to the presence of abundant functional groups on La/N-CQDs, which could be uniformly dispersed in the epoxy matrix, effectively filling the void defects formed during the curing process due to the evaporation of water molecules, thus enhancing the internal density of the coating. Compared to pure EP, the incorporation of La/N-CQDs imparts better bonding strength between the coating and the metal substrate, while also improving the corrosion resistance of the coating. In contrast, the adhesion strength of the La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coating composite slightly decreased, which may be attributed to the excessive agglomeration of La/N-CQDs.

The EIS test allows numerical evaluation of the corrosion process of the coatings during the corrosion experiments, so the EIS test was used to evaluate the corrosion resistance of the studied coating system under the immersion conditions of 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, and the EIS data were fitted by the ZSimpWin software using the electrical equivalent circuit in Fig. 6 .The results were shown in Fig. 7 which showed the effect of different La/N-CQDs content on the corrosion resistance of the coatings. A complete capacitive resistance arc could be observed from the Nyquist image, indicating that the coating was equivalent to a complete capacitive circuit. The radius of the capacitive resistance arc tended to increase with the addition of carbon quantum dots content (0 wt%–2 wt%). This was because the highly dispersed carbon quantum dot particles improved the densification of the epoxy resin coating to a certain extent, filled the micropores produced by the epoxy resin during solidification, and played an important role in inhibiting the corrosive medium. As the content of carbon quantum dots increased to 2.5 wt%, the radius of the arc tolerance began to shrink, and too many carbon quantum dots were enriched in the epoxy resin, and the phenomenon of agglomeration occurs. This led to a decrease in the dispersion of carbon quantum dots and the densification of the coating structure. In addition, the protective properties of the pure EP and La/N-CQDs@EP composite coatings decreased significantly with increasing immersion time, which was characterized by a decrease in the low-frequency impedance modulus (|Z|0.01 Hz). For the pure EP coating, |Z|0.01 Hz decreased dramatically by an order of magnitude after only 3 days of immersion, indicated a rapid decomposition of the coating in the presence of the corrosive medium. After a period of immersion, the corrosive medium slowly penetrated internally along the coating defects until it came into contact with the substrate, leading to the onset of the corrosion reaction. The La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating exhibited a high Z' of more than 5 × 105 Ω·cm2 after 7 days of immersion and stayed around 6 × 104 Ω·cm2 after 14 days of immersion. La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coatings exhibited the highest |Z|0.01 Hz values in corrosion testing, an order of magnitude higher than the other contents.

The Bode plot exhibited a pattern similar to that of the Nyquist plot. It was evident that all composite coatings were primarily characterized by excellent physical barrier properties after 1 day of immersion. However, following 5 days of immersion, the La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP coatings transition into a different protective mode (warburg), the emergence of warburg impedance indicated that the controlling step of the corrosion reaction has transformed from the charge transfer step to the diffusion step. This mode predominantly occurs at low frequencies between 102 and 10−2 Hz, which was attributed to the rapid passage of the corrosive medium through the coating substrate, leading to corrosion processes at the coating/NdFeB substrate interface. After 7 days of immersion, the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating also adopted a protection model based on low-frequency control. Throughout the entire period, the predominant protection mechanism was a physical barrier, which effectively shielded against corrosion-related substance.

After a 7-day immersion test, the Nyquist plots of all composite coatings displayed concave capacitance semicircles at high frequencies and linear responses at low frequencies. This observation indicated a significant increase in the diameter of the capacitance semicircle for the composite coatings containing La/N-CQDs, especially with a doping level of 2 wt%. This suggested that an appropriate amount of La/N-CQDs could effectively enhance the corrosion resistance of the coatings and inhibit charge transfer.

In order to investigate the as-prepared coatings' corrosion resistant property in-depth, the EIS data was fitted using the electrical equivalent circuit showed in the Fig. 6 through the ZSimpWin software. The fitting results were listed in Table 1 . Rc and Rct represented the coating and charge transfer resistances, respectively. Qc and Qdl corresponded to the coating constant phase element and the double-layer constant phase element, respectively. Qdl and Rct were related to the corrosion reaction at the coating/neodymium iron boron interface. The former signified the delamination of the coating and the initiation of corrosion at the coating/neodymium iron boron interface in situ, while the latter was associated with the rate of the corrosion reaction.

From Table 1, it could be seen that the Qc and Qdl values of all coatings increased after soaking, while the Rc and Rct values decreased. This was consistent with the changes in the capacitive arc and |Z|0.01Hz. Furthermore, after prolonged soaking, the La/N-CQDs@EP composite coating exhibited significantly higher Rc values compared to the pure EP coating, demonstrating that a small amount of doped carbon quantum dots could enhance the cross-linking density of the coating, which was beneficial for preventing the penetration of corrosive media. Among the La/N-CQDs@EP composite coatings, the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating had the highest Rc value compared to the pure EP coating. This indicated that doping with carbon quantum dots significantly improved the barrier performance of the coating. Notably, after soaking for 14 days, the Qdl value of the pure EP coating was much higher than that of the composite coatings, while its Rct value was much lower than that of the composite coatings (Table 1). The La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating demonstrated lower Qdl values and the highest Rct values during the long soaking process compared to the other composite coatings (Table 1). These phenomena indicated that the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating exhibited the best anticorrosive performance.

The electrochemical polarization curves of pure EP and composite coatings with varying carbon quantum dot contents, after 14 days of immersion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, were presented in Fig. 8 . The gray curve represented the potential polarization of bare NdFeB after 0 days of immersion, exhibiting a corrosion potential (E
corr ) of −0.823 V. The corrosion potential under pure EP protection was measured at −0.802 V. In comparison to the pure EP coating, the corrosion potentials of the composite coatings doped with different amounts of carbon quantum dots display a positive shift, indicating a significant enhancement in the corrosion resistance of the composite coatings. Notably, the corrosion potential of the coating with 2 wt% carbon quantum dots was more positive than that of the other coatings, and it also exhibited the lowest corrosion current density. Therefore, the 2 wt% coating demonstrated the best corrosion resistance in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, which was consistent with the findings from the Nyquist and Bode plots.

The cathodic Tafel slope, anodic Tafel slope, corrosion potential and corrosion current density correspond to βc, βa, E
corr and i
corr , respectively (Table 2 ).

EDS observations were conducted to analyze the surface morphology of composite coatings on epoxy-coated NdFeB substrates after 14 days of immersion with varying La/N-CQDs contents. As illustrated in Fig. 9 , numerous corrosion products were observed on the surfaces of NdFeB coated with both pure EP and La/N-CQDs0.5 % EP coatings, indicating significant damage and severe corrosion of the substrates (Fig. 9a, b). In contrast, the surfaces of the La/N-CQDs1 %@EP and La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coatings exhibited bulging, with the former displaying a more pronounced bulge compared to the latter, which shown only slight undulation and relatively fewer corrosion products (Fig. 9c, f). This result suggested that an appropriate amount of carbon quantum dots could enhance the corrosion protection capabilities of the epoxy coatings, while excessive doping led to agglomeration, reducing coating densification and thereby impairing protective efficacy. The surface of the pure EP coating system revealed a wide array of cracks and voids, primarily due to the semi-permeable nature of the organic coating. Under 3.5 wt% NaCl immersion, certain corrosive agents could easily penetrate through surface defects into the coating's interior, causing a significant accumulation of Cl−, H₂O, and O₂ in these defects. This accumulation could trigger the expansion of micro-defects and exacerbate coating failure. In contrast, carbon quantum dots in composite coatings effectively fill the pores and defects formed during the curing process, allowing the surface to remain relatively intact even after prolonged immersion.

Among the various composite coatings, the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating exhibited the smoothest surface. Consequently, it could be concluded that the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating demonstrates the highest corrosion resistance among all coatings investigated, which aligned with the EIS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology after peeling off the coatings
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the NdFeB substrate was analyzed using SEM and SPM following the removal of the coating. After completing the electrochemical experiments, the coating on the NdFeB was removed, allowing for observation of the resulting corrosion morphology via SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the pure EP coating and the composite coating exhibited markedly different protective effects after immersion. The pure EP coating displayed a significant corrosion response when immersed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 14 days, characterized by the formation of a large white layer of loose rust and numerous depressions on the metal surface (Fig. 10a). These observations indicated a substantial deterioration in the coating's barrier performance, likely attributable to the aggressive nature of the immersion medium. In contrast, for substrates coated with nanofiller/epoxy coatings, the metal oxidation reaction was notably inhibited, with a significant reduction in corrosion products observed as the concentration of doped carbon quantum dots increased (Fig. 10e). The La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating exhibited excellent corrosion resistance in harsh environments, as evidenced by the absence of visible corrosion products on the substrate's surface. The surfaces of NdFeB substrates coated with different coatings to corrosion in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution were analyzed and characterized using SPM images. The results obtained by SPM demonstrated the surface roughness of NdFeB substrates subjected to corrosion under various coatings. After a 14-day immersion period, it was evident that the surface of the NdFeB substrate has become significantly rough. The surface roughness of the NdFeB substrate under pure EP coating protection was approximately 286 μm, the highest among all specimens. Following the application of the composite coating, surface roughness decreased with increasing concentrations of carbon quantum dots. The lowest surface roughness value was recorded for the NdFeB substrate protected by the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating (Fig. 11e). However, the higher roughness observed for the NdFeB substrate under the La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP coating may be attributed to excess carbon quantum dots, leading to agglomeration. This phenomenon was consistent with the SEM corrosion morphology analysis (Fig. 11f).

The corrosion protection mechanism for all coatings was illustrated in Fig. 12 . Due to the rapid evaporation of water and solvents during the formation of pure EP coatings, numerous micropores were generated on the surface of pure EP. The aforementioned micropores provided a rapid invasion path for corrosive media. The ingress of oxygen and corrosive media into the defects in the EP coating allowed the initiation of anodic and cathodic reactions at the metal substrate surface. The cathodic reaction results in the production of hydroxyl radicals (Fig. 12c), while the corresponding anodic reaction gives rise to the formation of Nd3+ and Fe3+ ions, which in turn led to severe corrosion [3]. Once localized corrosion has formed, it disrupted the original equilibrium, thereby promoting further corrosion. The incorporation of carbon quantum dots into EP coatings not only rectified the defects inherent to the coating process but also facilitated the formation of a more robust epoxy resin network. This was achieved through the reinforcement of hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, and covalent bonding between the carbon quantum dots and the epoxy resin matrix. This results in a notable enhancement in the structural density of the EP coatings. Concurrently, the carbon quantum dots could be effectively adsorbed on the uncoated NdFeB via the coordination bond between the heteroatoms and the metal matrix. Furthermore, the La/N-CQDs could be partially dissolved in water, thereby releasing lanthanum cations and organic compounds. Subsequently, the released lanthanum ion (La3+) reacted with the hydroxyl anion (OH−), resulting in the formation of a film on the substrate that exerted a certain barrier ability [39]. The fundamental mechanism underlying corrosion resistance was the chemical adsorption and coordination between organic corrosion inhibitors and metal surfaces. This chemisorption was due to the fact that N, O and other heteroatoms with a lone pair of electrons were able to readily fill the empty 3d orbitals of iron atoms, thereby forming coordination covalent bonds between the heteroatoms and the metal surfaces [22]. As the quantity of doped carbon quantum dots increases, any excess carbon quantum dots will agglomerate within the coating, which will result in a reduction in the protective properties of the composite coating.

The friction and wear properties of the materials were evaluated using a reciprocating friction and wear tester (MFT-R4000, China) under atmospheric conditions and at room temperature. A 3 mm silicon nitride ball was employed, with a sliding distance of 5 mm, a normal load of 5 N, a duration of 30 min, and a sliding frequency of 5 Hz. As shown in Fig. 14a, the coefficient of friction (COF) curves for all coatings exhibited a notable increase, which could be attributed to the micro-bumping of the contact surface. As the friction process progresses, the COF gradually stabilizes and remains steady until the conclusion of the test, likely due to the expansion of the contact area. In Fig. 14, the tribological properties of the EP composites doped with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are superior to those of the pure EP coating. The average COF value for the pure EP was 0.4, while the mean COF values of the various composite coatings doped with CQDs shown a slight decrease. The composite coatings containing 1.5 % CQDs exhibited the lowest mean COF value of 0.23. Furthermore, the mean COF value of the composite coatings doped with CQDs decreased with an increase in the CQD content up to a certain point. However, the COF values of the composite coatings initially declined and then increased with a further rise in the CQD content. At higher concentrations of CQDs, agglomeration causes the micro-concave structure of the coating to become more pronounced, resulting in the formation of pits. This, in turn, led to an increase in the average COF value of the coating.

To gain further insight into the wear behavior and mechanisms of the EP composites, the wear surfaces were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and a two-dimensional profilometer, as shown in Fig. 13 . The morphology and width of the wear traces, determined by SEM, revealed that all the coated surfaces exhibited prominent ploughing traces, indicative of severe wear. The surface morphology of these traces suggested that the predominant wear mechanisms were adhesive wear and fatigue wear. The maximum width of the wear trace on the pure EP coating was 1292 μm (Fig. 13a), which decreased and then increased with increasing carbon quantum dot doping content. In contrast, the shortest width of the composite coating was 684.3 μm at 1.5 % content (Fig. 13d). This observation was supported by the distribution of adhesive debris on the surface of the composite coating, consistent with the characteristics of the adhesive wear mechanism. The cross-sectional profiles of the wear marks were characterized by 2D profilometry, and Fig. 15 showed the wear rates of the composites at varying doping contents, exhibiting a trend similar to that of the average COF. The wear rate of the pristine epoxy was 6.64×10-4 mm3/N·m, indicating severe wear and poor abrasion resistance. Compared to the pure EP coating, the La/N-CQDs0.5 %@EP, La/N-CQDs1 %@EP, La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP, La/N-CQDs2 %@EP, and La/N-CQDs2.5 %@EP composites showed reductions in wear rate by 34.9 %, 43.1 %, 67 %, 47.7 %, and 33.5 %, respectively. The wear rate of the composites at 1.5 % content was notably lower than that of the composites at other concentrations, indicating that the La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP composite exhibited the highest wear resistance, which was consistent with the SEM results.

Fig. 16 showed the friction mechanism of all coatings. Fig. 17 illustrated that C, O, N, La, Fe, Nd and other elements were distributed uniformly on the wear surface. The majority of C and O originate from the epoxy resin, while N and La were derived from the synthesized La/N-CQDs. The presence of Fe and Nd could be attributed to the NdFeB substrate. The excellent wear resistance of the composite coating could be attributed to the presence of a multitude of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots. These functional groups were capable of adsorbing onto the positively charged sites that were presented on the friction front, thereby forming a carbon-based lubrication film (Fig. 16b). This film then reacted with the abraded surface, effectively acting as a ‘bearing ball’ to prevent further wear. The transfer film formed at the interface between the silicon carbide ball and the composite coating could transform the wear of the ‘silicon carbide ball-polymer composite’ into the wear of the ‘polymer-polymer composite’. This avoided direct contact between the silicon carbide ball and the EP composite coating, and transformed the sliding friction into rolling friction. The conversion of sliding friction to rolling friction played an important role in the anti-wear experiments. The agglomeration of excessively added carbon quantum dots in the EP matrix results in the degradation of mechanical properties and the formation of stress concentrations in the EP composites. This, in turn, led to a weakening of the tribological properties of the lubricants, which reflected the commonality of abrasive wear induced by excessive nano-additives. This phenomenon represented the primary cause of the degradation of wear resistance.

In summary, La/N-CQDs were successfully synthesized using the hydrothermal method and employed to prepare the La/N-CQDs@EP composite coatings on NdFeB substrates through the rotary coating method. The results of the electrochemical tests, corrosion product assessments, surface morphology evaluations, and friction and wear tests could be summarized as follows:
1.
The successful synthesis of La/N-CQDs and the presence of functional groups, including hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surface, which enhance the cross-linking density of the epoxy resin, were characterized using FTIR, UV–vis, and XPS. The excellent dispersibility of La/N-CQDs in water was confirmed by TEM.
2.
The incorporation of La/N-CQDs could enhance the structural density of the epoxy resin and impede the penetration of corrosive media. The impedance modulus of the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating is two orders of magnitude higher than that of the pure EP coatings after 14 days of immersion, demonstrating its effectiveness in providing corrosion protection.
3.
The addition of La/N-CQDs nanofillers significantly contributes to the friction reduction and wear resistance of the La/N-CQDs2 %@EP coating. The average friction coefficient of La/N-CQDs1.5 %@EP coating is as low as μ = 0.23, and the wear rate is recorded at 2.19 × 10-4 mm3/N·m under dry friction conditions, representing reductions of 42.5 % and 67 %, respectively, compared to pure EP coating.

Qi Zeng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Haitao Yin: Methodology, Formal analysis. Qunfang Li: Writing – review & editing. Ihor I. Bulyk: Writing – review & editing. Zhixiang Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Shengguo Zhou: Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52065025), and the Key Research and Development Program of Jiangxi Province (20224BBE51041).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.surfcoat.2025.132108.
